question_en: Which statements about dynamic programming, divide and conquer, and greedy algorithms are true?

choices.C: Only statements I and II are true.
choices.D: Only statements II and III are true.

correct_answer_en: Only statements I and II are true.
explanation_en: The dynamic-programming statement is true because it solves small subproblems, stores partial solutions, and combines them to solve larger problems.

question_en: MergeSort, Kruskal's minimum spanning tree algorithm, and Floyd-Warshall are examples of which algorithmic paradigms, respectively?

choices.A: Greedy, dynamic programming, divide and conquer
choices.B: Divide and conquer, dynamic programming, greedy
choices.C: Greedy, divide and conquer, dynamic programming
choices.D: Dynamic programming, divide and conquer, greedy
choices.E: Divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming

correct_answer_en: Divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming
explanation_en: Floyd-Warshall is dynamic programming because it builds shortest-path solutions by progressively allowing more intermediate vertices.

question_en: Which statements about algorithm analysis are correct? I. Dynamic programming can make some exponential recursive algorithms efficient. II. Trial-and-error algorithms are impractical with recursion because they are exhaustive. III. Recursive algorithms always run faster than iterative versions. IV. Binary search trees are suitable for recursive problems.

choices.A: Only I and II are correct.
choices.B: Only I and IV are correct.
choices.C: Only II and III are correct.
choices.D: Only III and IV are correct.

correct_answer_en: Only I and IV are correct.
explanation_en: Dynamic programming can avoid repeated recursive work, and binary trees naturally support recursive processing. Recursion is not automatically faster, and trial-and-error is not universally impractical.

question_en: Which algorithm is commonly used to compute shortest paths between all pairs of vertices and is based on dynamic programming?
explanation_en: Floyd-Warshall uses a dynamic-programming recurrence over allowed intermediate vertices to compute all-pairs shortest paths.
